subject: social-studies
title: Grade 5 Social Studies — United States History
description: Texas Grade 5 SS follows TEKS §113.16 — U.S. History from Native Americans through the 20th century at a survey level. Six core strands: First Americans, Colonization, Revolution, Westward Expansion, Civil War, 20th-Century America. Authored 2026-05-17.

lessons.title: Native American Cultures Before 1492
lessons.objective: Identify major Native American culture regions and adaptations to environment.

questions.prompt: Native Americans first reached North America from Asia by:
choices: Crossing a land bridge that connected Asia and Alaska during the last Ice Age
Coming from Europe in the year 1000
questions.answer: Crossing a land bridge that connected Asia and Alaska during the last Ice Age
questions.explanation: Scientists believe Native Americans' ancestors crossed Beringia — a land bridge connecting Asia and Alaska — at least 15,000 years ago, when sea levels were lower during the Ice Age. Some may also have traveled by sea along the coast.

questions.prompt: The Plains tribes (like the Lakota and Cheyenne) depended heavily on:
choices: Buffalo (bison) for food, clothing, shelter, and tools
questions.answer: Buffalo (bison) for food, clothing, shelter, and tools
questions.explanation: Plains tribes used nearly every part of the buffalo. Meat for food, hides for clothes and tipis, bones for tools, sinew for thread. When American expansion nearly wiped out buffalo herds in the 1800s, it devastated Plains cultures.

questions.prompt: The Eastern Woodland tribes (like the Iroquois) often lived in:
choices: Longhouses made of wood and bark, in fortified villages
Tipis they took down each day
questions.answer: Longhouses made of wood and bark, in fortified villages
questions.explanation: Eastern Woodland tribes built longhouses — long bark-covered wooden buildings where extended families lived. The Iroquois Confederacy was a powerful alliance of five tribes (later six) in present-day New York.

questions.prompt: The "Three Sisters" planted together by many Native American farmers were:
questions.answer: Corn, beans, and squash
questions.explanation: Corn, beans, and squash were grown together in the same mound because they help each other: corn provides a stalk for beans to climb, beans add nitrogen to the soil, and squash leaves shade the ground (keeping moisture in). This was one of the most successful farming systems in the world.

questions.prompt: Roughly how long had Native Americans been living in the Americas before Europeans arrived?
questions.answer: Tens of thousands of years
questions.explanation: Native peoples had lived in the Americas for at least 15,000 years before Columbus arrived in 1492 — vastly longer than Europeans had been in Europe. During that time they developed hundreds of distinct languages, cultures, and complex societies (Maya, Aztec, Inca, Mississippian, and many more).

lessons.title: Columbus, Colonization & Early America
lessons.objective: Identify key explorers and the founding of the 13 colonies.

questions.prompt: Christopher Columbus's voyages (starting 1492) led to:
choices: The discovery of an empty continent
European contact with peoples and lands they hadn't known about — beginning centuries of exploration, colonization, and cultural exchange (and conflict)
The end of all Native American cultures within a year
questions.answer: European contact with peoples and lands they hadn't known about — beginning centuries of exploration, colonization, and cultural exchange (and conflict)
questions.explanation: Columbus didn't discover an empty land — millions of people already lived in the Americas. But his voyages connected the "Old World" (Europe, Asia, Africa) with the "New World" (Americas), starting a massive exchange of plants, animals, diseases, and people known as the Columbian Exchange — with huge consequences for both worlds.

questions.prompt: Why were European diseases SO deadly to Native Americans in the 1500s?
choices: Native Americans had no immune systems
Native Americans had no resistance to European diseases like smallpox because they had never been exposed before
The diseases were created in labs
questions.answer: Native Americans had no resistance to European diseases like smallpox because they had never been exposed before
questions.explanation: Smallpox, measles, and other diseases that Europeans carried killed up to 90% of Native populations in some regions. Native immune systems had never encountered these diseases. This catastrophe — alongside warfare and forced labor — collapsed many Native societies.

questions.prompt: The 13 English colonies were divided into three regions. Which region was known for big plantations growing cash crops like tobacco and rice?
questions.answer: Southern Colonies
questions.explanation: The Southern Colonies (Virginia, Maryland, the Carolinas, Georgia) had warm climates and fertile soil ideal for tobacco, rice, indigo, and later cotton. These plantations expanded the brutal system of slavery.

questions.prompt: The Pilgrims sailed to America on which ship in 1620?
questions.answer: Mayflower
questions.explanation: The Pilgrims left England (via the Netherlands) on the Mayflower, seeking religious freedom. They landed at Plymouth, Massachusetts, in November 1620 and signed the Mayflower Compact — an early example of self-government.

questions.prompt: The first Thanksgiving (1621) was a harvest celebration between:
choices: Pilgrims and Wampanoag people, who had helped the Pilgrims survive their first year
questions.answer: Pilgrims and Wampanoag people, who had helped the Pilgrims survive their first year
questions.explanation: Without help from the Wampanoag — especially Squanto, who taught them how to plant corn — half the Pilgrims who came on the Mayflower died in their first winter. The 1621 harvest feast was shared with about 90 Wampanoag, and is the basis for the American Thanksgiving holiday.

lessons.title: Revolution, Independence & Founding Documents
lessons.objective: Identify key events and documents of the American Revolution.

questions.prompt: The Boston Tea Party (1773) was a protest by colonists against:
choices: British taxes on tea — colonists dumped 342 chests of tea into Boston Harbor
questions.answer: British taxes on tea — colonists dumped 342 chests of tea into Boston Harbor
questions.explanation: Disguised as Mohawk Indians, about 60 colonists boarded three ships and dumped £10,000 worth of British tea into Boston Harbor. They were protesting the Tea Act, which they saw as taxation without representation. Britain responded harshly — which only united the colonies more.

questions.prompt: The Declaration of Independence was adopted on:
questions.answer: July 4, 1776
questions.explanation: The Continental Congress adopted the Declaration of Independence on July 4, 1776 — declaring the 13 colonies free and independent from Britain. That's why Americans celebrate Independence Day every July 4th.

questions.prompt: Who was the commander of the Continental Army during the Revolutionary War — and later became the first U.S. President?
questions.answer: George Washington
questions.explanation: Washington led the Continental Army through the long, often desperate war — including the famous winter at Valley Forge. After victory, he was unanimously elected the first U.S. president in 1789, setting precedents that shaped the office to this day.

questions.prompt: Why was the Battle of Saratoga (1777) a turning point in the Revolution?
choices: It was the largest battle of the war
American victory there convinced France to officially ally with the United States, providing money, soldiers, and a navy
questions.answer: American victory there convinced France to officially ally with the United States, providing money, soldiers, and a navy
questions.explanation: Before Saratoga, France had quietly helped the Americans. After Saratoga proved Americans could beat the British, France openly allied — sending troops, ships, and a fleet that proved decisive at the Battle of Yorktown (1781), the war's final major engagement.

questions.prompt: The U.S. Constitution, ratified in 1788, replaced what earlier governing document?
questions.answer: The Articles of Confederation
questions.explanation: The Articles of Confederation (1781) was the first U.S. constitution, but it made the central government too weak. After several crises showed the system wasn't working, delegates met at the Constitutional Convention in 1787 and wrote the U.S. Constitution we still use today.

lessons.title: Louisiana Purchase, Oregon Trail & Manifest Destiny
lessons.objective: Identify major events and consequences of westward expansion.

questions.prompt: Thomas Jefferson's Louisiana Purchase (1803) from France:
choices: Doubled the size of the United States for about 3 cents an acre
Was returned to France a year later
questions.answer: Doubled the size of the United States for about 3 cents an acre
questions.explanation: Jefferson bought 828,000 square miles for $15 million — roughly 3 cents per acre, one of the best real-estate deals in history. The territory included parts of 15 future U.S. states from Louisiana to Montana.

questions.prompt: Lewis and Clark led an expedition (1804-1806) to:
choices: Find a Northwest Passage to Asia
Explore the Louisiana Purchase, map the land, study plants and animals, and reach the Pacific Ocean
questions.answer: Explore the Louisiana Purchase, map the land, study plants and animals, and reach the Pacific Ocean
questions.explanation: Jefferson sent Meriwether Lewis and William Clark west to map the new territory and find a route to the Pacific. They were guided crucially by Sacagawea, a Shoshone woman who helped them navigate and translate. The journey produced enormous knowledge about western lands and peoples.

questions.prompt: The Oregon Trail, used by settlers from the 1840s through the 1880s, ran roughly from:
choices: Independence, Missouri to the Oregon Country (around 2,000 miles)
questions.answer: Independence, Missouri to the Oregon Country (around 2,000 miles)
questions.explanation: About 400,000 settlers walked or rode wagons along the Oregon Trail from Missouri to the Pacific Northwest — a journey of about 2,000 miles that took 4-6 months. Many died from disease, accidents, and the difficult terrain.

questions.prompt: The California Gold Rush (1849) caused:
choices: California's population to grow rapidly as people came seeking gold — leading to California becoming a state in 1850
The end of immigration to America
The fall of the U.S. dollar
questions.answer: California's population to grow rapidly as people came seeking gold — leading to California becoming a state in 1850
questions.explanation: When gold was found at Sutter's Mill in 1848, hundreds of thousands of "forty-niners" rushed to California. The territory's population exploded — and California was admitted as the 31st state in 1850, becoming the first state west of the Rockies.

questions.prompt: Westward expansion brought devastating consequences for:
choices: Native Americans, who were forced from their lands, suffered diseases, and saw their populations and cultures collapse
questions.answer: Native Americans, who were forced from their lands, suffered diseases, and saw their populations and cultures collapse
questions.explanation: As settlers moved west, the U.S. government forced Native tribes off ancestral lands (the Trail of Tears, Indian Removal Act, reservation system). Buffalo populations were nearly wiped out, destroying Plains cultures. Disease, warfare, and broken treaties devastated Native populations across the continent.

lessons.title: Slavery, Civil War & Reconstruction
lessons.objective: Identify the causes, key events, and aftermath of the Civil War.

questions.prompt: The MAIN cause of the Civil War was:
choices: Slavery — the South wanted to keep and expand it; many in the North wanted to limit or end it
questions.answer: Slavery — the South wanted to keep and expand it; many in the North wanted to limit or end it
questions.explanation: Slavery was the central cause. Southern economy and society were built on enslaved labor. When Lincoln's election in 1860 signaled the federal government would limit slavery's expansion, Southern states seceded. The Confederate states' own declarations of secession explicitly cited slavery as the reason.

questions.prompt: Abraham Lincoln, the U.S. President during the Civil War, is most famous for:
choices: Issuing the Emancipation Proclamation and preserving the Union
questions.answer: Issuing the Emancipation Proclamation and preserving the Union
questions.explanation: Lincoln led the country through its darkest crisis. His Emancipation Proclamation (1863) declared enslaved people in Confederate areas free, and the war he led preserved the Union. He was assassinated in April 1865, just days after the war ended.

questions.prompt: Juneteenth (June 19) commemorates:
choices: The day enslaved people in Texas learned they were free, in 1865
The day the Civil War started
questions.answer: The day enslaved people in Texas learned they were free, in 1865
questions.explanation: On June 19, 1865, federal troops arrived in Galveston, Texas, and announced that all enslaved people in the state were free — over two years after the Emancipation Proclamation. Juneteenth ("June" + "Nineteenth") has been celebrated ever since; it became a U.S. federal holiday in 2021.

questions.prompt: The 13th Amendment to the U.S. Constitution (1865):
choices: Gave women the right to vote
Abolished slavery in the United States
questions.answer: Abolished slavery in the United States
questions.explanation: The 13th Amendment, ratified at the end of 1865, abolished slavery throughout the United States — making the Emancipation Proclamation's freedom permanent and applying it to every state, not just the former Confederacy.

questions.prompt: After the Civil War, Reconstruction (1865-1877) tried to:
choices: Rebuild the South and integrate newly freed Black Americans into society as citizens with full rights
Send the Confederacy back into the Union by force
questions.answer: Rebuild the South and integrate newly freed Black Americans into society as citizens with full rights
questions.explanation: Reconstruction was a 12-year effort to repair the South, give former enslaved people citizenship and voting rights, and rebuild the country. It achieved important constitutional amendments (13th, 14th, 15th) but ended without finishing — leaving many rights unenforced until the 20th-century Civil Rights Movement.

lessons.title: Industrial America, World Wars & Civil Rights
lessons.objective: Identify major 20th-century developments at a survey level.

questions.prompt: The late 1800s and early 1900s saw the U.S. become an industrial nation. Which invention transformed American work and life?
choices: Electricity, the automobile, and the airplane (all invented or popularized in this era)
questions.answer: Electricity, the automobile, and the airplane (all invented or popularized in this era)
questions.explanation: Edison's lightbulb (1879), Ford's affordable Model T car (1908), and the Wright Brothers' first powered flight (1903) all came during this transformative period. Cities grew, factories multiplied, and the economy shifted from farming to industry.

questions.prompt: World War II (1939-1945) ended when:
choices: Germany surrendered in May 1945, and Japan surrendered in August 1945 after atomic bombs were dropped on Hiroshima and Nagasaki
questions.answer: Germany surrendered in May 1945, and Japan surrendered in August 1945 after atomic bombs were dropped on Hiroshima and Nagasaki
questions.explanation: WWII ended in two phases: Germany surrendered in May 1945 ("V-E Day" — Victory in Europe). Japan held out, but surrendered in August 1945 after the U.S. dropped atomic bombs on Hiroshima and Nagasaki. The war killed over 60 million people worldwide — the deadliest conflict in history.

questions.prompt: Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. led which major American movement?
choices: The Civil Rights Movement of the 1950s-1960s, using nonviolent protest to fight racial segregation
The labor union movement of the 1880s
questions.answer: The Civil Rights Movement of the 1950s-1960s, using nonviolent protest to fight racial segregation
questions.explanation: Inspired by Mahatma Gandhi, Dr. King led nonviolent protests — marches, boycotts, sit-ins — to challenge Jim Crow segregation. His leadership helped pass the Civil Rights Act (1964) and the Voting Rights Act (1965). He was assassinated in 1968.

questions.prompt: The 19th Amendment (1920) gave the right to vote to:
questions.answer: Women
questions.explanation: After a 70-year campaign by suffragists like Susan B. Anthony and Elizabeth Cady Stanton, the 19th Amendment guaranteed women the right to vote in all U.S. elections. (Black men had won voting rights with the 15th Amendment in 1870, though many states blocked their voting until the 1960s.)

questions.prompt: The United States landed astronauts on the Moon for the first time in:
questions.explanation: On July 20, 1969, Apollo 11 astronauts Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin became the first humans to walk on the Moon. Armstrong's words — "That's one small step for man, one giant leap for mankind" — are among the most famous in history. Houston's Johnson Space Center was Mission Control.
